Entropic Regularization: Sinkhorn Algorithm

Entropic regularization makes optimal transport smooth, strictly convex and scalable. This chapter first explains the discrete KL-regularized problem, derives Sinkhorn’s alternating matrix scaling algorithm, and then rewrites the same construction as a relative-entropy projection problem. It then records the general continuous formulation, explains the path-space Schrodinger problem behind the static coupling formulation, develops the dual soft-transform picture, and presents the main convex regularization variants and the debiased Sinkhorn divergence.

The presentation connects the older matrix-scaling literature Sinkhorn, 1964Sinkhorn & Knopp, 1967Sinkhorn, 1967 with modern entropic OT Cuturi, 2013Peyré & Cuturi, 2019.

Guiding Comparison

The unregularized Kantorovich problem is a linear program. Entropy replaces it by a smooth strictly convex problem whose optimizer has the scaling form P=diag⁡(u)Kdiag⁡(v)P=\operatorname{diag}(u)K\operatorname{diag}(v)P=diag(u)Kdiag(v). The cost is a biased OT approximation, but the payoff is differentiability and matrix-vector iterations.

from pathlib import Path
import sys

from IPython.display import Image as DisplayImage
from IPython.display import display

here = Path.cwd()
myst_dir = None
for candidate in [here, here.parent, here / "myst", here.parent / "myst", here.parent.parent / "myst"]:
    if (candidate / "ot4ml_web.py").exists():
        myst_dir = candidate.resolve()
        sys.path.insert(0, str(myst_dir))
        break

if myst_dir is None:
    raise RuntimeError("Could not locate myst/ot4ml_web.py")

repo_root = myst_dir.parent
thumbnails = repo_root / "notebooks-figures" / "thumbnails"

def show_book_figure(name, width=760):
    display(DisplayImage(filename=str(thumbnails / f"{name}.png"), width=width))

Entropic Regularization for Discrete Measures¶

Entropy turns a possibly non-unique linear program into a unique smooth problem. The price is bias, but the reward is differentiability and fast scaling algorithms.

Definition: Discrete Shannon--Boltzmann Entropy

For a nonnegative matrix PPP, its Shannon--Boltzmann entropy is

H(P):=−∑i,jPi,jlog⁡Pi,j,H(P) \eqdef -\sum_{i,j}P_{i,j}\log P_{i,j},H(P):=−i,j∑​Pi,j​logPi,j​,
(1)

with the convention 0log⁡0=00\log 0=00log0=0.

Using this entropy as a regularizing function gives the approximate transport value

LCϵ(a,b):=min⁡P∈U(a,b)⟨P,C⟩−ϵH(P).\mathcal{L}_{C}^{\epsilon}(a,b) \eqdef \min_{P\in\mathbf{U}(a,b)} \langle P,C\rangle - \epsilon H(P).LCϵ​(a,b):=P∈U(a,b)min​⟨P,C⟩−ϵH(P).
(2)

Equivalently, the regularizer is ϵ∑i,jPi,jlog⁡Pi,j\epsilon\sum_{i,j}P_{i,j}\log P_{i,j}ϵ∑i,j​Pi,j​logPi,j​. It penalizes concentrated couplings and makes the objective strictly convex on the relative interior of the transport polytope.

Proposition: Existence and Uniqueness of Entropic OT

Assume that a,ba,ba,b are probability histograms and that CCC is finite. For every ϵ>0\epsilon>0ϵ>0, problem (2) admits a unique minimizer. If all entries of aaa and bbb are positive, then this minimizer is positive on every entry.

Proof

The transport polytope is non-empty and compact, and the objective is continuous with the convention 0log⁡0=00\log0=00log0=0, so a minimizer exists. On the relative interior,

−∂2H(P)=diag⁡(1/Pi,j)-\partial^2 H(P)=\operatorname{diag}(1/P_{i,j})−∂2H(P)=diag(1/Pi,j​)
(3)

is positive definite on every non-zero feasible direction. Hence −H-H−H is strictly convex on the polytope, which gives uniqueness.

If ai,bj>0a_i,b_j>0ai​,bj​>0 and a minimizer had Pi,j=0P_{i,j}=0Pi,j​=0, then the perturbation Pt=(1−t)P+t a⊗bP_t=(1-t)P+t\,a\otimes bPt​=(1−t)P+ta⊗b remains feasible for small t>0t>0t>0. The derivative of rlog⁡rr\log rrlogr at zero along a positive direction is −∞-\infty−∞, so the objective decreases, contradicting optimality.

Smoothing Effect¶

The entropy acts as a barrier for positivity and makes LCϵ(a,b)\mathcal{L}_{C}^{\epsilon}(a,b)LCϵ​(a,b) smooth in aaa, bbb, and CCC as long as these variables stay in the relative interior. As ϵ→+∞\epsilon\to+\inftyϵ→+∞, the minimizer converges to the independent coupling a⊗ba\otimes ba⊗b; as ϵ→0\epsilon\to0ϵ→0, it approaches the optimal face of the original transport linear program.

show_book_figure("sinkhorn-entropy-lp-geometry")

Entropic regularization and slack barriers. Large ϵ\epsilonϵ selects an interior reference point, while small ϵ\epsilonϵ moves the minimizer toward a low-cost face of the transport polytope. The second row gives the analogous entropy-on-slacks picture for a generic linear program.

Entropy Barriers Versus Generic LP Barriers¶

For a generic linear program min⁡zℓ⊤z\min_z \ell^\top zminz​ℓ⊤z with constraints Az≤bAz\le bAz≤b, one can introduce positive slacks s=b−Azs=b-Azs=b−Az and penalize them by an entropy. This is a useful analogy, but it is not the standard self-concordant interior-point barrier. The canonical barrier is the Burg, or reverse-KL, barrier −∑ilog⁡si-\sum_i\log s_i−∑i​logsi​, which leads to Newton systems.

Optimal transport is special because entropy is placed on the entries of PPP, while the constraints are only row and column marginals. This separable structure turns Bregman projections into diagonal rescalings, giving the Sinkhorn iterations.

Sinkhorn’s Algorithm¶

Sinkhorn’s algorithm is alternating normalization of rows and columns. The key point is that the optimizer of the entropic problem has a multiplicative scaling form.

Proposition: Scaling Form of Entropic OT

PPP is the unique solution of (2) if and only if there exist nonnegative vectors u∈R+nu\in\RR_+^nu∈R+n​ and v∈R+mv\in\RR_+^mv∈R+m​ such that

Pi,j=uiKi,jvj,Ki,j:=exp⁡(−Ci,j/ϵ),P_{i,j}=u_iK_{i,j}v_j, \qquad K_{i,j}\eqdef\exp(-C_{i,j}/\epsilon),Pi,j​=ui​Ki,j​vj​,Ki,j​:=exp(−Ci,j​/ϵ),
(4)

and P∈U(a,b)P\in\mathbf{U}(a,b)P∈U(a,b).

Proof

After removing zero-mass rows and columns, the minimizer is strictly positive, so the positivity constraint can be ignored in the first-order conditions. Introduce Lagrange multipliers f∈Rnf\in\RR^nf∈Rn and g∈Rmg\in\RR^mg∈Rm for the two marginal constraints. The Lagrangian is

L(P,f,g)=⟨P,C⟩+ϵ∑i,jPi,jlog⁡Pi,j+⟨f,a−P1⟩+⟨g,b−P⊤1⟩.\mathcal{L}(P,f,g) = \langle P,C\rangle + \epsilon\sum_{i,j}P_{i,j}\log P_{i,j} + \langle f,a-P\mathbf 1\rangle + \langle g,b-P^\top\mathbf 1\rangle .L(P,f,g)=⟨P,C⟩+ϵi,j∑​Pi,j​logPi,j​+⟨f,a−P1⟩+⟨g,b−P⊤1⟩.
(5)

Stationarity with respect to Pi,jP_{i,j}Pi,j​ gives

Ci,j+ϵ(log⁡Pi,j+1)−fi−gj=0.C_{i,j} + \epsilon(\log P_{i,j}+1) - f_i-g_j =0.Ci,j​+ϵ(logPi,j​+1)−fi​−gj​=0.
(6)

Thus Pi,j=exp⁡((fi+gj−Ci,j)/ϵ−1)P_{i,j}=\exp((f_i+g_j-C_{i,j})/\epsilon-1)Pi,j​=exp((fi​+gj​−Ci,j​)/ϵ−1), which is exactly the scaling form after absorbing the one-body terms into uuu and vvv.

In matrix notation, P=diag⁡(u)Kdiag⁡(v)P=\operatorname{diag}(u)K\operatorname{diag}(v)P=diag(u)Kdiag(v). The marginal constraints become

u⊙(Kv)=a,v⊙(K⊤u)=b.u\odot(Kv)=a, \qquad v\odot(K^\top u)=b.u⊙(Kv)=a,v⊙(K⊤u)=b.
(7)

Solving each equation in turn gives Sinkhorn’s algorithm:

u(ℓ+1)=aKv(ℓ),v(ℓ+1)=bK⊤u(ℓ+1).u^{(\ell+1)} = \frac{a}{Kv^{(\ell)}}, \qquad v^{(\ell+1)} = \frac{b}{K^\top u^{(\ell+1)}}.u(ℓ+1)=Kv(ℓ)a​,v(ℓ+1)=K⊤u(ℓ+1)b​.
(8)

The division is entrywise. The scaling vectors are not unique: multiplying uuu by λ>0\lambda>0λ>0 and vvv by 1/λ1/\lambda1/λ leaves PPP unchanged.

show_book_figure("sinkhorn-marginal-errors")

Marginal constraints during Sinkhorn scaling. Row normalizations align the red source marginal and leave a blue defect; column normalizations align the blue target marginal and leave a red defect.

The interactive demo exposes the alternating row/column normalization directly. Change the half-step count to see the current coupling acquire one marginal, lose the other, and then converge toward both.

show_book_figure("sinkhorn-continuous-marginal-scaling")

Dense Sinkhorn scaling for one-dimensional Gaussian-mixture marginals. The violet side curves are the current row and column sums; the red and blue curves are the prescribed marginals.

After convergence, the regularization strength controls how much of the Gibbs kernel remains visible in the optimal plan. Small ϵ\epsilonϵ produces a concentrated transport band, while larger ϵ\epsilonϵ spreads the same marginals into a smoother coupling.

show_book_figure("sinkhorn-coupling-iterations")

Final Sinkhorn couplings for the same one-dimensional marginals and four regularization strengths. Decreasing ϵ\epsilonϵ sharpens the plan toward an optimal-transport graph; increasing ϵ\epsilonϵ keeps more of the product structure.

show_book_figure("sinkhorn-potentials-iterations")

KL-normalized dual potentials along the scaling iteration. The logarithmic scaling potentials stabilize as the row/column normalizations converge.

The next interactive demo keeps the iteration count high and varies the temperature. It is the quickest way to see the geometry-bias tradeoff: low temperature is geometric and sharp, high temperature is smooth and closer to independence.

Complexity bounds for Sinkhorn and comparisons with accelerated first-order methods are discussed in Altschuler et al., 2017Dvurechensky et al., 2018Knight, 2008. For a dense n×mn\times mn×m problem, each iteration costs one multiplication by KKK and one by K⊤K^\topK⊤, so the cost scales like CnmCnmCnm for CCC iterations. For fixed positive ϵ\epsilonϵ, the marginal error eventually has a linear regime, but small ϵ\epsilonϵ makes the Gibbs kernel more peaked and scaling harder.

show_book_figure("sinkhorn-linear-rate-epsilon")

Marginal violation along Sinkhorn half-steps for several values of ϵ\epsilonϵ. Smaller ϵ\epsilonϵ gives sharper transport geometry but slower scaling.

Remark: Separable Gaussian Kernels on Grids

When samples lie on a Cartesian grid and c(x,y)=∥x−y∥2c(x,y)=\norm{x-y}^2c(x,y)=∥x−y∥2, the Gibbs kernel is Gaussian and factorizes along coordinates:

K(x,y)=exp⁡(−∥x−y∥2ϵ)=∏ℓ=1dexp⁡(−(xℓ−yℓ)2ϵ).K(x,y) = \exp\left(-\frac{\norm{x-y}^2}{\epsilon}\right) = \prod_{\ell=1}^d \exp\left(-\frac{(x_\ell-y_\ell)^2}{\epsilon}\right).K(x,y)=exp(−ϵ∥x−y∥2​)=ℓ=1∏d​exp(−ϵ(xℓ​−yℓ​)2​).
(9)

If the grid has qqq points per axis and N=qdN=q^dN=qd points total, multiplication by KKK can be applied one coordinate direction at a time. A direct dense one-dimensional multiplication costs O(q2)O(q^2)O(q2) on each of the qd−1q^{d-1}qd−1 coordinate lines and is repeated for ddd axes, giving O(dqd+1)=O(dN1+1/d)O(dq^{d+1})=O(dN^{1+1/d})O(dqd+1)=O(dN1+1/d) per half-step instead of O(N2)O(N^2)O(N2).

Reformulation Using Relative Entropy¶

The KL formulation identifies Sinkhorn as a projection method. It also prepares the continuous and unbalanced settings, where a reference measure is essential.

Definition: Discrete Relative Entropy

For nonnegative matrices P,QP,QP,Q of the same size, the generalized relative entropy is

KL⁡(P∣Q):=∑i,jPi,jlog⁡Pi,jQi,j−Pi,j+Qi,j.\operatorname{KL}(P|Q) \eqdef \sum_{i,j} P_{i,j}\log\frac{P_{i,j}}{Q_{i,j}} - P_{i,j} + Q_{i,j}.KL(P∣Q):=i,j∑​Pi,j​logQi,j​Pi,j​​−Pi,j​+Qi,j​.
(10)

The convention is 0log⁡0=00\log0=00log0=0, and KL⁡(P∣Q)=+∞\operatorname{KL}(P|Q)=+\inftyKL(P∣Q)=+∞ if Qi,j=0Q_{i,j}=0Qi,j​=0 but Pi,j>0P_{i,j}>0Pi,j​>0 for some entry.

For matrices with the same total mass, the affine terms cancel and

KL⁡(P∣Q)=∑i,jPi,jlog⁡Pi,jQi,j.\operatorname{KL}(P|Q) = \sum_{i,j}P_{i,j}\log\frac{P_{i,j}}{Q_{i,j}}.KL(P∣Q)=i,j∑​Pi,j​logQi,j​Pi,j​​.
(11)

On fixed-mass couplings, taking Q=1n×mQ=\mathbf 1_{n\times m}Q=1n×m​ is equivalent to subtracting the Shannon--Boltzmann entropy. Taking the tensor-product reference gives the normalized formulation

min⁡P∈U(a,b)⟨P,C⟩+ϵKL⁡(P∣a⊗b).\min_{P\in\mathbf U(a,b)} \langle P,C\rangle + \epsilon\operatorname{KL}(P|a\otimes b).P∈U(a,b)min​⟨P,C⟩+ϵKL(P∣a⊗b).
(12)
Proposition: Relative Entropy is Distance-Like

Let P,Q∈R+n×mP,Q\in\RR_+^{n\times m}P,Q∈R+n×m​ have the same total mass and assume Qi,j>0Q_{i,j}>0Qi,j​>0 on the support of PPP. Then KL⁡(P∣Q)≥0\operatorname{KL}(P|Q)\ge0KL(P∣Q)≥0, with equality if and only if P=QP=QP=Q.

Proof

Write ϕ(s)=slog⁡s−s+1\phi(s)=s\log s-s+1ϕ(s)=slogs−s+1. Convexity gives ϕ(s)≥ϕ(1)+ϕ′(1)(s−1)=0\phi(s)\ge\phi(1)+\phi'(1)(s-1)=0ϕ(s)≥ϕ(1)+ϕ′(1)(s−1)=0, with equality only at s=1s=1s=1. Hence

KL⁡(P∣Q)=∑i,jQi,jϕ(Pi,j/Qi,j)≥0.\operatorname{KL}(P|Q) = \sum_{i,j}Q_{i,j}\phi(P_{i,j}/Q_{i,j}) \ge0.KL(P∣Q)=i,j∑​Qi,j​ϕ(Pi,j​/Qi,j​)≥0.
(13)
Proposition: Reference Measure Shift for KL

After removing zero-mass rows and columns, assume a,a′∈Δna,a'\in\simplex_na,a′∈Δn​ and b,b′∈Δmb,b'\in\simplex_mb,b′∈Δm​ have positive entries. For every P∈U(a,b)P\in\mathbf U(a,b)P∈U(a,b),

KL⁡(P∣a⊗b)=KL⁡(P∣a′⊗b′)−KL⁡(a∣a′)−KL⁡(b∣b′).\operatorname{KL}(P|a\otimes b) = \operatorname{KL}(P|a'\otimes b') - \operatorname{KL}(a|a') - \operatorname{KL}(b|b').KL(P∣a⊗b)=KL(P∣a′⊗b′)−KL(a∣a′)−KL(b∣b′).
(14)

Consequently, for fixed positive marginals, changing the positive tensor-product reference only adds a constant on the transport polytope.

Proof

Expand the logarithm and use the marginal constraints:

KL⁡(P∣a⊗b)=KL⁡(P∣a′⊗b′)+∑iailog⁡ai′ai+∑jbjlog⁡bj′bj=KL⁡(P∣a′⊗b′)−KL⁡(a∣a′)−KL⁡(b∣b′).\begin{aligned} \operatorname{KL}(P|a\otimes b) &= \operatorname{KL}(P|a'\otimes b') + \sum_i a_i\log\frac{a_i'}{a_i} + \sum_j b_j\log\frac{b_j'}{b_j} \\ &= \operatorname{KL}(P|a'\otimes b') - \operatorname{KL}(a|a') - \operatorname{KL}(b|b'). \end{aligned}KL(P∣a⊗b)​=KL(P∣a′⊗b′)+i∑​ai​logai​ai′​​+j∑​bj​logbj​bj′​​=KL(P∣a′⊗b′)−KL(a∣a′)−KL(b∣b′).​
(15)

The tensor-product reference is nevertheless useful when supports vary. It makes explicit which entries may vanish and passes cleanly to the continuous formulation.

show_book_figure("sinkhorn-dual-potentials-epsilon")

KL-normalized Sinkhorn dual potentials for one-dimensional Gaussian-mixture histograms. Increasing ϵ\epsilonϵ turns the hard ccc-transform geometry into smoother log-sum-exp potentials.

Proposition: Convergence with ϵ\epsilonϵ

Assume, after removing zero-mass rows and columns, that aaa and bbb are positive and that CCC is finite. The unique solution PϵP_\epsilonPϵ​ converges, as ϵ→0\epsilon\to0ϵ→0, to the maximum-entropy solution among all optimal solutions of the Kantorovich problem. Moreover,

Pϵ→a⊗bas ϵ→+∞.P_\epsilon \to a\otimes b \qquad \text{as } \epsilon\to+\infty.Pϵ​→a⊗bas ϵ→+∞.
(16)
Proof Sketch

For ϵ→0\epsilon\to0ϵ→0, use compactness of the transport polytope and compare the optimality inequalities for the entropic problem against an exact Kantorovich optimizer. The cost gap is bounded by ϵ\epsilonϵ times a KL difference, so every cluster point is cost-optimal; after dividing by ϵ\epsilonϵ, the cluster point is the KL-minimizer on the optimal face.

For ϵ→+∞\epsilon\to+\inftyϵ→+∞, subtract a constant from CCC so that C≥0C\ge0C≥0. Testing the objective at a⊗ba\otimes ba⊗b gives

KL⁡(Pϵ∣a⊗b)≤⟨C,a⊗b⟩ϵ,\operatorname{KL}(P_\epsilon|a\otimes b) \le \frac{\langle C,a\otimes b\rangle}{\epsilon},KL(Pϵ​∣a⊗b)≤ϵ⟨C,a⊗b⟩​,
(17)

so the KL divergence to a⊗ba\otimes ba⊗b vanishes.

show_book_figure("sinkhorn-plan-epsilon")

Entropically regularized couplings between the red disk and blue annulus point clouds. The plans are strictly positive for every ϵ>0\epsilon>0ϵ>0, but the visible mass pattern evolves from nearly radial and sparse to diffuse as ϵ\epsilonϵ increases.

General Formulation¶

The continuous formulation replaces matrices by measures and discrete KL by relative entropy. For probability measures α\alphaα and β\betaβ, define

Lcϵ(α,β):=min⁡π∈Π(α,β)∫X×Yc(x,y)  dπ(x,y)+ϵKL⁡(π∣α⊗β).\mathcal{L}_{c}^{\epsilon}(\alpha,\beta) \eqdef \min_{\pi\in\Couplings(\alpha,\beta)} \int_{\X\times\Y}c(x,y)\,\d\pi(x,y) + \epsilon\operatorname{KL}(\pi|\alpha\otimes\beta).Lcϵ​(α,β):=π∈Π(α,β)min​∫X×Y​c(x,y)dπ(x,y)+ϵKL(π∣α⊗β).
(18)
Definition: Relative Entropy of Measures

For nonnegative measures π\piπ and ξ\xiξ on X×Y\X\times\YX×Y,

KL⁡(π∣ξ):=∫X×Ylog⁡( dπ dξ(x,y)) dπ(x,y)+∫X×Y( dξ− dπ).\operatorname{KL}(\pi|\xi) \eqdef \int_{\X\times\Y} \log\left(\frac{\d\pi}{\d\xi}(x,y)\right)\d\pi(x,y) + \int_{\X\times\Y}(\d\xi-\d\pi).KL(π∣ξ):=∫X×Y​log(dξdπ​(x,y))dπ(x,y)+∫X×Y​(dξ−dπ).
(19)

By convention, KL⁡(π∣ξ)=+∞\operatorname{KL}(\pi|\xi)=+\inftyKL(π∣ξ)=+∞ if π\piπ is not absolutely continuous with respect to ξ\xiξ.

For fixed balanced marginals, the specific product reference only matters up to additive constants, provided the reference marginals are mutually absolutely continuous with α\alphaα and β\betaβ. Its support still matters: it determines which couplings have finite entropy.

Path-Space Schrodinger Problem¶

Schrodinger’s reciprocal problem is naturally posed on paths rather than on endpoint pairs. The Sinkhorn problem appears after the path law is reduced to its two endpoint marginals.

Unregularized Path-Space Transport¶

Let Ω=C([0,1];X)\Omega=C([0,1];\X)Ω=C([0,1];X) be a path space and let et(ω)=ωte_t(\omega)=\omega_tet​(ω)=ωt​ be the evaluation maps. Given a path action A:Ω→[0,+∞]\mathcal A:\Omega\to[0,+\infty]A:Ω→[0,+∞], the unregularized path-space problem is

inf⁡M∈P(Ω){∫ΩA(ω)  dM(ω):(e0)♯M=α, (e1)♯M=β}.\inf_{M\in\mathcal P(\Omega)} \left\{ \int_\Omega\mathcal A(\omega)\,\d M(\omega) : (e_0)_\sharp M=\alpha,\ (e_1)_\sharp M=\beta \right\}.M∈P(Ω)inf​{∫Ω​A(ω)dM(ω):(e0​)♯​M=α, (e1​)♯​M=β}.
(20)

For quadratic Wasserstein geometry on Rd\RR^dRd,

A(ω)={∫01∥ω˙t∥2  dt,ω absolutely continuous,+∞,otherwise.\mathcal A(\omega) = \begin{cases} \int_0^1\norm{\dot\omega_t}^2\,\d t, & \omega\text{ absolutely continuous},\\ +\infty, & \text{otherwise}. \end{cases}A(ω)={∫01​∥ω˙t​∥2dt,+∞,​ω absolutely continuous,otherwise.​
(21)

The endpoint cost induced by the action is

cA(x,y):=inf⁡{A(ω):e0(ω)=x, e1(ω)=y}.c_{\mathcal A}(x,y) \eqdef \inf\left\{ \mathcal A(\omega): e_0(\omega)=x,\ e_1(\omega)=y \right\}.cA​(x,y):=inf{A(ω):e0​(ω)=x, e1​(ω)=y}.
(22)

For the quadratic action, the minimizing path is the straight segment and cA(x,y)=∥x−y∥2c_{\mathcal A}(x,y)=\norm{x-y}^2cA​(x,y)=∥x−y∥2.

Proposition: Endpoint Reduction of Path-Space Transport

Assume that minimizing paths can be selected measurably, or approximated by measurable selections. Then the path-space problem has the same value as

inf⁡π∈Π(α,β)∫X×XcA(x,y)  dπ(x,y).\inf_{\pi\in\Couplings(\alpha,\beta)} \int_{\X\times\X}c_{\mathcal A}(x,y)\,\d\pi(x,y).π∈Π(α,β)inf​∫X×X​cA​(x,y)dπ(x,y).
(23)

If π⋆\pi^\starπ⋆ is an optimal endpoint coupling and ωx,y\omega^{x,y}ωx,y is an optimal path from xxx to yyy, then

M⋆=∫X×Xδωx,y  dπ⋆(x,y)M^\star = \int_{\X\times\X}\delta_{\omega^{x,y}}\,\d\pi^\star(x,y)M⋆=∫X×X​δωx,y​dπ⋆(x,y)
(24)

is an optimal path law.

Proof

For any feasible path law MMM, set π=(e0,e1)♯M\pi=(e_0,e_1)_\sharp Mπ=(e0​,e1​)♯​M. Then π∈Π(α,β)\pi\in\Couplings(\alpha,\beta)π∈Π(α,β) and by definition of cAc_{\mathcal A}cA​,

∫ΩA(ω)  dM(ω)≥∫X×XcA(x,y)  dπ(x,y).\int_\Omega\mathcal A(\omega)\,\d M(\omega) \ge \int_{\X\times\X}c_{\mathcal A}(x,y)\,\d\pi(x,y).∫Ω​A(ω)dM(ω)≥∫X×X​cA​(x,y)dπ(x,y).
(25)

Conversely, mix selected minimizing paths according to any coupling π\piπ and then optimize over π\piπ.

Entropic Path-Space Problem¶

Let Rϵ∈P(Ω)R^\epsilon\in\mathcal P(\Omega)Rϵ∈P(Ω) be a reference path law, for instance a Brownian or Langevin dynamics at noise level ϵ\epsilonϵ. The dynamic Schrodinger bridge problem is the entropy projection

SBϵ(α,β):=inf⁡M∈P(Ω){ϵKL⁡(M∣Rϵ):(e0)♯M=α, (e1)♯M=β}.\mathrm{SB}_\epsilon(\alpha,\beta) \eqdef \inf_{M\in\mathcal P(\Omega)} \left\{ \epsilon\operatorname{KL}(M|R^\epsilon): (e_0)_\sharp M=\alpha,\ (e_1)_\sharp M=\beta \right\}.SBϵ​(α,β):=M∈P(Ω)inf​{ϵKL(M∣Rϵ):(e0​)♯​M=α, (e1​)♯​M=β}.
(26)

It asks for the most likely path law, relative to the prior dynamics, among all path laws matching the observed endpoints Schrödinger, 1931Léonard, 2012Léonard, 2014Chen et al., 2016.

The dynamic problem also has viscous Benamou--Brenier formulations. In one common convention,

∂tρt+div⁡(ρtvt)=σ2Δρt\partial_t\rho_t+\operatorname{div}(\rho_t v_t) = \frac{\sigma}{2}\Delta\rho_t∂t​ρt​+div(ρt​vt​)=2σ​Δρt​
(27)

and one minimizes a kinetic action. After absorbing the diffusion into the velocity ut=vt−σ2∇log⁡ρtu_t=v_t-\frac{\sigma}{2}\nabla\log\rho_tut​=vt​−2σ​∇logρt​, the same minimizers are obtained from

∫01 ⁣∫(12∥ut(x)∥2+σ28∥∇log⁡ρt(x)∥2)ρt(x)  dx  dt,\int_0^1\!\int \left( \frac12\norm{u_t(x)}^2 + \frac{\sigma^2}{8}\norm{\nabla\log\rho_t(x)}^2 \right) \rho_t(x)\,\d x\,\d t,∫01​∫(21​∥ut​(x)∥2+8σ2​∥∇logρt​(x)∥2)ρt​(x)dxdt,
(28)

up to endpoint entropy terms. The extra term is a Fisher-information penalty.

Proposition: Endpoint Reduction of the Schrodinger Problem

Assume regular conditional laws exist and that the relative-entropy chain rule applies. If R01ϵ=(e0,e1)♯RϵR^\epsilon_{01}=(e_0,e_1)_\sharp R^\epsilonR01ϵ​=(e0​,e1​)♯​Rϵ, then

SBϵ(α,β)=inf⁡π∈Π(α,β)ϵKL⁡(π∣R01ϵ).\mathrm{SB}_\epsilon(\alpha,\beta) = \inf_{\pi\in\Couplings(\alpha,\beta)} \epsilon\operatorname{KL}(\pi|R^\epsilon_{01}).SBϵ​(α,β)=π∈Π(α,β)inf​ϵKL(π∣R01ϵ​).
(29)

For a fixed endpoint coupling π\piπ, the minimizing path law is the mixture of reference bridges

Mπ=∫Rϵ,x,y  dπ(x,y).M^\pi = \int R^{\epsilon,x,y}\,\d\pi(x,y).Mπ=∫Rϵ,x,ydπ(x,y).
(30)
Proof

Disintegrate both MMM and RϵR^\epsilonRϵ with respect to their endpoint pairs. The chain rule gives

KL⁡(M∣Rϵ)=KL⁡(π∣R01ϵ)+∫KL⁡(Mx,y∣Rϵ,x,y)  dπ(x,y).\operatorname{KL}(M|R^\epsilon) = \operatorname{KL}(\pi|R^\epsilon_{01}) + \int \operatorname{KL}(M^{x,y}|R^{\epsilon,x,y}) \,\d\pi(x,y).KL(M∣Rϵ)=KL(π∣R01ϵ​)+∫KL(Mx,y∣Rϵ,x,y)dπ(x,y).
(31)

The second term is nonnegative and vanishes exactly when the conditional path law is the reference bridge for π\piπ-almost every endpoint pair.

For Brownian reference dynamics on Rd\RR^dRd, the endpoint prior has heat-kernel density proportional to

exp⁡(−∥x−y∥2ϵ).\exp\left(-\frac{\norm{x-y}^2}{\epsilon}\right).exp(−ϵ∥x−y∥2​).
(32)

After rewriting this prior with respect to α⊗β\alpha\otimes\betaα⊗β, the endpoint problem is exactly the continuous Sinkhorn problem up to an additive constant. Sinkhorn computes which endpoints should be paired; the path-space Schrodinger bridge then connects each pair by a Brownian bridge.

show_book_figure("sinkhorn-path-space-bridges")

Endpoint couplings lifted to Brownian bridges. Increasing ϵ\epsilonϵ both softens the endpoint coupling and amplifies the Brownian fluctuations between paired endpoints.

Definition: Mutual Information

If (X,Y)∼π(X,Y)\sim\pi(X,Y)∼π have marginals X∼αX\sim\alphaX∼α and Y∼βY\sim\betaY∼β, their mutual information is

I(X,Y):=KL⁡(π∣α⊗β).\mathcal I(X,Y) \eqdef \operatorname{KL}(\pi|\alpha\otimes\beta).I(X,Y):=KL(π∣α⊗β).
(33)

It is nonnegative and vanishes if and only if XXX and YYY are independent.

With this terminology, the entropic problem is

inf⁡X∼α,  Y∼βE(c(X,Y))+ϵI(X,Y).\inf_{X\sim\alpha,\;Y\sim\beta} \mathbb E(c(X,Y))+\epsilon\mathcal I(X,Y).X∼α,Y∼βinf​E(c(X,Y))+ϵI(X,Y).
(34)

Large ϵ\epsilonϵ favors nearly independent endpoints, while small ϵ\epsilonϵ suppresses endpoint randomness and recovers an optimal Monge--Kantorovich coupling in the limit.

Dual of Sinkhorn¶

The dual point of view replaces couplings by potentials and soft ccc-transforms. It is the right formulation for stabilized implementations and differentiation.

Discrete Dual¶

The KL-normalized problem has the dual

min⁡P∈U(a,b)⟨P,C⟩+ϵKL⁡(P∣a⊗b)=max⁡f,g[⟨f,a⟩+⟨g,b⟩−ϵ∑i,jexp⁡(fi+gj−Ci,jϵ)aibj+ϵ].\min_{P\in\mathbf U(a,b)} \langle P,C\rangle+\epsilon\operatorname{KL}(P|a\otimes b) = \max_{f,g} \left[ \langle f,a\rangle+\langle g,b\rangle - \epsilon\sum_{i,j} \exp\left(\frac{f_i+g_j-C_{i,j}}{\epsilon}\right)a_i b_j + \epsilon \right].P∈U(a,b)min​⟨P,C⟩+ϵKL(P∣a⊗b)=f,gmax​[⟨f,a⟩+⟨g,b⟩−ϵi,j∑​exp(ϵfi​+gj​−Ci,j​​)ai​bj​+ϵ].
(35)

The optimal potentials are linked to the scaling variables through

ui=aiefi/ϵ,vj=bjegj/ϵ.u_i=a_i e^{f_i/\epsilon}, \qquad v_j=b_j e^{g_j/\epsilon}.ui​=ai​efi​/ϵ,vj​=bj​egj​/ϵ.
(36)

Discrete Soft ccc-Transforms¶

For fixed ggg, maximizing the dual with respect to fff gives

fi=−ϵlog⁡∑jexp⁡(gj−Ci,jϵ)bj.f_i = -\epsilon\log \sum_j \exp\left(\frac{g_j-C_{i,j}}{\epsilon}\right)b_j.fi​=−ϵlogj∑​exp(ϵgj​−Ci,j​​)bj​.
(37)

This is a smoothed minimum.

Definition: Soft-Min and Discrete Soft ccc-Transform

For h∈Rmh\in\RR^mh∈Rm and weights b∈Δmb\in\simplex_mb∈Δm​,

min⁡bϵ(h):=−ϵlog⁡∑je−hj/ϵbj.\min_b^\epsilon(h) \eqdef -\epsilon\log\sum_j e^{-h_j/\epsilon}b_j.bminϵ​(h):=−ϵlogj∑​e−hj​/ϵbj​.
(38)

It converges to min⁡jhj\min_j h_jminj​hj​ as ϵ→0\epsilon\to0ϵ→0. Given a cost matrix CCC, the discrete soft ccc-transforms are

fi=min⁡bϵ(Ci,⋅−g),gj=min⁡aϵ(C⋅,j−f).f_i=\min_b^\epsilon(C_{i,\cdot}-g), \qquad g_j=\min_a^\epsilon(C_{\cdot,j}-f).fi​=bminϵ​(Ci,⋅​−g),gj​=aminϵ​(C⋅,j​−f).
(39)

Exponentiating the alternating soft-transform iterations recovers Sinkhorn’s algorithm. For small ϵ\epsilonϵ, one must compute the log-sum-exp terms with the usual stabilization trick: subtract the minimum before exponentiating and add it back afterward.

show_book_figure("sinkhorn-soft-c-transform-epsilon")

Soft ccc-transforms for decreasing temperatures. A positive ϵ\epsilonϵ replaces the hard lower envelope by a log-sum-exp soft minimum.

Continuous Dual and Soft-Transforms¶

For compact spaces and continuous cost, the KL-regularized problem has the concave dual

Lcϵ(α,β)=sup⁡f,gDϵ(f,g),\mathcal{L}_{c}^{\epsilon}(\alpha,\beta) = \sup_{f,g} \mathcal D_\epsilon(f,g),Lcϵ​(α,β)=f,gsup​Dϵ​(f,g),
(40)

where

Dϵ(f,g)=∫f  dα+∫g  dβ−ϵ∫(e(f(x)+g(y)−c(x,y))/ϵ−1) dα(x) dβ(y).\mathcal D_\epsilon(f,g) = \int f\,\d\alpha+\int g\,\d\beta - \epsilon \int \left( e^{(f(x)+g(y)-c(x,y))/\epsilon} - 1 \right) \d\alpha(x)\d\beta(y).Dϵ​(f,g)=∫fdα+∫gdβ−ϵ∫(e(f(x)+g(y)−c(x,y))/ϵ−1)dα(x)dβ(y).
(41)

This is the smooth counterpart of the hard feasibility constraint f⊕g≤cf\oplus g\le cf⊕g≤c from the Kantorovich dual.

Definition: Continuous Soft ccc-Transforms

For f∈C(X)f\in\Cc(\X)f∈C(X) and g∈C(Y)g\in\Cc(\Y)g∈C(Y),

fc,ϵ(y):=−ϵlog⁡∫Xe(f(x)−c(x,y))/ϵ dα(x),f^{c,\epsilon}(y) \eqdef -\epsilon\log \int_\X e^{(f(x)-c(x,y))/\epsilon} \d\alpha(x),fc,ϵ(y):=−ϵlog∫X​e(f(x)−c(x,y))/ϵdα(x),
(42)

and

gcˉ,ϵ(x):=−ϵlog⁡∫Ye(g(y)−c(x,y))/ϵ dβ(y).g^{\bar c,\epsilon}(x) \eqdef -\epsilon\log \int_\Y e^{(g(y)-c(x,y))/\epsilon} \d\beta(y).gcˉ,ϵ(x):=−ϵlog∫Y​e(g(y)−c(x,y))/ϵdβ(y).
(43)
Proposition: Existence and Uniqueness of Entropic Dual Potentials

Assume X\XX and Y\YY are compact and ccc is continuous. The dual problem has solutions, and the set of solutions is

(f⋆+λ,g⋆−λ),λ∈R.(f^\star+\lambda,g^\star-\lambda), \qquad \lambda\in\RR .(f⋆+λ,g⋆−λ),λ∈R.
(44)
Proof Sketch

Normalize potentials by imposing ∫f  dα=0\int f\,\d\alpha=0∫fdα=0. Replacing a pair of potentials by the corresponding soft transforms does not decrease the dual objective. The transformed potentials have oscillations bounded by the oscillation of ccc, and their modulus of continuity is controlled by the modulus of continuity of ccc. Arzela--Ascoli gives existence.

Uniqueness up to constants follows from strict convexity of H↦∫eH/ϵ d(α⊗β)H\mapsto\int e^{H/\epsilon}\d(\alpha\otimes\beta)H↦∫eH/ϵd(α⊗β) on the image of (f,g)↦f⊕g−c(f,g)\mapsto f\oplus g-c(f,g)↦f⊕g−c, modulo constants.

Remark: Convexity Properties of Soft Transforms

For the bilinear cost c(x,y)=−⟨x,y⟩c(x,y)=-\langle x,y\ranglec(x,y)=−⟨x,y⟩, the soft transform preserves concavity. For the quadratic cost c(x,y)=∥x−y∥2/2c(x,y)=\norm{x-y}^2/2c(x,y)=∥x−y∥2/2, optimal potentials have the form f⋆(x)=∥x∥2/2−ϕ⋆(x)f^\star(x)=\norm{x}^2/2-\phi^\star(x)f⋆(x)=∥x∥2/2−ϕ⋆(x) and g⋆(y)=∥y∥2/2−ψ⋆(y)g^\star(y)=\norm{y}^2/2-\psi^\star(y)g⋆(y)=∥y∥2/2−ψ⋆(y), where ϕ⋆\phi^\starϕ⋆ and ψ⋆\psi^\starψ⋆ are convex.

Remark: Gaussian Marginals

For c(x,y)=∥x−y∥2c(x,y)=\norm{x-y}^2c(x,y)=∥x−y∥2 and Gaussian marginals, soft transforms preserve quadratic functions because products and convolutions of Gaussian functions remain Gaussian. Hence the optimal entropic potentials are quadratic and the optimal entropic coupling is Gaussian.

Other Convex Regularizers¶

KL regularization is the case that leads to multiplicative Sinkhorn scalings. Replacing KL by another density-ratio penalty keeps the same transport constraints but changes the scalar law linking the optimal density to the dual potentials.

Let ϕ\phiϕ be an entropy function and define

Lc,ϕϵ(α,β):=min⁡π∈Π(α,β)∫c(x,y)  dπ(x,y)+ϵDϕ(π∣α⊗β).\mathcal L_{c,\phi}^{\epsilon}(\alpha,\beta) \eqdef \min_{\pi\in\Couplings(\alpha,\beta)} \int c(x,y)\,\d\pi(x,y) + \epsilon D_\phi(\pi|\alpha\otimes\beta).Lc,ϕϵ​(α,β):=π∈Π(α,β)min​∫c(x,y)dπ(x,y)+ϵDϕ​(π∣α⊗β).
(45)
Proposition: Dual and Density Law for ϕ\phiϕ-Regularized OT

Under standard Fenchel--Rockafellar qualification assumptions,

Lc,ϕϵ(α,β)=sup⁡f,g∫f  dα+∫g  dβ−ϵ∫ϕ∗,≥0(f(x)+g(y)−c(x,y)ϵ) dα(x) dβ(y).\mathcal L_{c,\phi}^{\epsilon}(\alpha,\beta) = \sup_{f,g} \int f\,\d\alpha+\int g\,\d\beta - \epsilon \int \phi^{*,\ge0} \left( \frac{f(x)+g(y)-c(x,y)}{\epsilon} \right) \d\alpha(x)\d\beta(y).Lc,ϕϵ​(α,β)=f,gsup​∫fdα+∫gdβ−ϵ∫ϕ∗,≥0(ϵf(x)+g(y)−c(x,y)​)dα(x)dβ(y).
(46)

If the optimal plan has density r⋆= dπ⋆/ d(α⊗β)r^\star=\d\pi^\star/\d(\alpha\otimes\beta)r⋆=dπ⋆/d(α⊗β) and the solution is smooth and interior, then

r⋆(x,y)=(ϕ′)−1(f⋆(x)+g⋆(y)−c(x,y)ϵ).r^\star(x,y) = (\phi')^{-1} \left( \frac{f^\star(x)+g^\star(y)-c(x,y)}{\epsilon} \right).r⋆(x,y)=(ϕ′)−1(ϵf⋆(x)+g⋆(y)−c(x,y)​).
(47)

For KL, ϕ(r)=rlog⁡r−r+1\phi(r)=r\log r-r+1ϕ(r)=rlogr−r+1 and ϕ∗,≥0(s)=es−1\phi^{*,\ge0}(s)=e^s-1ϕ∗,≥0(s)=es−1, recovering the Sinkhorn dual. Other choices replace the exponential law by another scalar transfer function:

ϕ(r)=rlog⁡r−r+1⇒r⋆=es,ϕ(r)=r−log⁡r−1⇒r⋆=(1−s)−1(s<1),ϕ(r)=12(r−1)2⇒r⋆=(1+s)+,s=f⋆⊕g⋆−cϵ.\begin{array}{lll} \phi(r)=r\log r-r+1 &\Rightarrow& r^\star=e^s,\\ \phi(r)=r-\log r-1 &\Rightarrow& r^\star=(1-s)^{-1}\quad(s<1),\\ \phi(r)=\frac12(r-1)^2 &\Rightarrow& r^\star=(1+s)_+, \end{array} \qquad s=\frac{f^\star\oplus g^\star-c}{\epsilon}.ϕ(r)=rlogr−r+1ϕ(r)=r−logr−1ϕ(r)=21​(r−1)2​⇒⇒⇒​r⋆=es,r⋆=(1−s)−1(s<1),r⋆=(1+s)+​,​s=ϵf⋆⊕g⋆−c​.
(48)
show_book_figure("sinkhorn-entropic-versus-quadratic-regularization")

Density-ratio regularizers and coupling support. KL gives the usual diffuse positive plan, the Burg barrier keeps positive but differently tailed support, and the quadratic density penalty can set entries exactly to zero through its positive-part law.

The interactive demo below separates the two effects. The left plot shows the pointwise law r=h(s)r=h(s)r=h(s), while the right plot recomputes a coupling after enforcing the marginals with that law. Changing ϵ\epsilonϵ controls how much the cost score is softened; changing the regularizer changes whether mass is spread everywhere, protected by a barrier, or clipped to a sparse support.

Bregman vs. ϕ\phiϕ-Divergence Regularization¶

The previous construction regularizes OT by a density-ratio divergence. This differs from using a Bregman divergence generated by a convex functional on the space of measures.

Definition: Measure Bregman Divergence

If Φ\PhiΦ is a differentiable convex functional on a convex class of nonnegative measures and ξ\xiξ is a reference measure, the Bregman divergence generated by Φ\PhiΦ is

BΦ(π∣ξ):=Φ(π)−Φ(ξ)−∫δΦ(ξ)  d(π−ξ),B_\Phi(\pi|\xi) \eqdef \Phi(\pi)-\Phi(\xi) - \int\delta\Phi(\xi)\,\d(\pi-\xi),BΦ​(π∣ξ):=Φ(π)−Φ(ξ)−∫δΦ(ξ)d(π−ξ),
(49)

where δΦ(ξ)\delta\Phi(\xi)δΦ(ξ) is the first variation.

Proposition: Dual Comparison

For a fixed product reference ξ=α⊗β\xi=\alpha\otimes\betaξ=α⊗β, Bregman regularization has a dual involving the global conjugate Φ∗\Phi^*Φ∗ and the translated dual coordinate δΦ(ξ)+(f⊕g−c)/ϵ\delta\Phi(\xi)+(f\oplus g-c)/\epsilonδΦ(ξ)+(f⊕g−c)/ϵ. In contrast, ϕ\phiϕ-regularization has the scalar-integral dual above and the pointwise density law

f⋆⊕g⋆−cϵ∈∂ϕ( dπ⋆ d(α⊗β)).\frac{f^\star\oplus g^\star-c}{\epsilon} \in \partial\phi \left( \frac{\d\pi^\star}{\d(\alpha\otimes\beta)} \right).ϵf⋆⊕g⋆−c​∈∂ϕ(d(α⊗β)dπ⋆​).
(50)
Main Idea of the Proof

Write the Bregman-regularized objective, up to constants independent of π\piπ, as

ϵΦ(π)+∫(c−ϵδΦ(ξ))  dπ.\epsilon\Phi(\pi) + \int(c-\epsilon\delta\Phi(\xi))\,\d\pi .ϵΦ(π)+∫(c−ϵδΦ(ξ))dπ.
(51)

Dualizing the marginal constraints and minimizing over π\piπ produces the global conjugate Φ∗\Phi^*Φ∗. Equality in Fenchel’s inequality gives the Bregman optimality condition. The density-ratio dual follows instead from the scalar Legendre formula for Dϕ(⋅∣α⊗β)D_\phi(\cdot|\alpha\otimes\beta)Dϕ​(⋅∣α⊗β).

Proposition: KL is the Common Bregman and ϕ\phiϕ Case

Under natural smoothness assumptions, if a Bregman divergence BΦ(α∣β)B_\Phi(\alpha|\beta)BΦ​(α∣β) equals a ϕ\phiϕ-divergence Dϕ(α∣β)D_\phi(\alpha|\beta)Dϕ​(α∣β) for all positive probability densities, then

ϕ(t)=c tlog⁡t+a(t−1)\phi(t)=c\,t\log t+a(t-1)ϕ(t)=ctlogt+a(t−1)
(52)

for some c≥0c\ge0c≥0 and a∈Ra\in\RRa∈R. Hence the common divergence is a multiple of KL, up to an irrelevant affine term.

Thus the two generalizations lead to different duals and different algorithms. Only for KL do density-ratio regularization and Bregman projection geometry coincide and reduce to multiplicative Sinkhorn scalings.

Sinkhorn Divergences¶

Sinkhorn divergences remove the entropic self-bias while retaining smoothness. They interpolate between OT-like geometry and kernel-like norms, which explains their statistical behavior.

Entropic Bias¶

The raw Sinkhorn cost is biased: for ϵ>0\epsilon>0ϵ>0, minimizing Lcϵ(α,β)\mathcal L_c^\epsilon(\alpha,\beta)Lcϵ​(α,β) over β\betaβ does not generally return β=α\beta=\alphaβ=α. In the large-temperature limit, the raw value behaves like a product interaction:

Proposition: Large-Temperature Entropic Bias

Assume ccc is bounded and continuous. Then

Lcϵ(α,β)→∬c(x,y)  dα(x) dβ(y)as ϵ→+∞.\mathcal L_c^\epsilon(\alpha,\beta) \to \iint c(x,y)\,\d\alpha(x)\d\beta(y) \qquad \text{as }\epsilon\to+\infty.Lcϵ​(α,β)→∬c(x,y)dα(x)dβ(y)as ϵ→+∞.
(53)

For c(x,y)=∥x−y∥2c(x,y)=\norm{x-y}^2c(x,y)=∥x−y∥2, minimizing this large-temperature limit over β\betaβ collapses toward a Dirac at the mean of α\alphaα.

Sinkhorn Divergences¶

The standard debiasing subtracts the two self-interaction energies. This cancellation removes the large-temperature attraction toward the independent coupling; positivity is a separate property, proved below through the positive-definite kernel associated with e−c/ϵe^{-c/\epsilon}e−c/ϵ.

Definition: Sinkhorn Divergence

For ϵ>0\epsilon>0ϵ>0, the debiased Sinkhorn divergence is

L‾cϵ(α,β):=Lcϵ(α,β)−12Lcϵ(α,α)−12Lcϵ(β,β).\overline{\mathcal L}_c^\epsilon(\alpha,\beta) \eqdef \mathcal L_c^\epsilon(\alpha,\beta) - \frac12\mathcal L_c^\epsilon(\alpha,\alpha) - \frac12\mathcal L_c^\epsilon(\beta,\beta).Lcϵ​(α,β):=Lcϵ​(α,β)−21​Lcϵ​(α,α)−21​Lcϵ​(β,β).
(54)
show_book_figure("sinkhorn-divergence-debiasing")

Debiasing by point optimization. For large ϵ\epsilonϵ, minimizing the raw entropic cost collapses atoms toward the barycenter, whereas the self-cost subtraction keeps a bimodal cloud.

The interactive demo below shows the same mechanism in a one-dimensional toy setting. Move the model cloud relative to the target and compare the raw entropic objective with its debiased version.

Lemma: Entropic Dual Cost at Optimum

Let (fα,β,gα,β)(f_{\alpha,\beta},g_{\alpha,\beta})(fα,β​,gα,β​) be optimal dual potentials. Then

Lcϵ(α,β)=∫fα,β  dα+∫gα,β  dβ.\mathcal L_c^\epsilon(\alpha,\beta) = \int f_{\alpha,\beta}\,\d\alpha + \int g_{\alpha,\beta}\,\d\beta.Lcϵ​(α,β)=∫fα,β​dα+∫gα,β​dβ.
(55)
Proof

At optimality,

1=∫e(fα,β(x)+gα,β(y)−c(x,y))/ϵ dβ(y)1 = \int e^{(f_{\alpha,\beta}(x)+g_{\alpha,\beta}(y)-c(x,y))/\epsilon} \d\beta(y)1=∫e(fα,β​(x)+gα,β​(y)−c(x,y))/ϵdβ(y)
(56)

for α\alphaα-almost every xxx. Therefore the exponential penalty term in the dual integrates to zero, and the dual value reduces to the two linear potential terms.

Proposition: Asymptotics of Sinkhorn Divergences

Assume the two measures are supported on the same space and that ccc is bounded, continuous, nonnegative, and satisfies c(x,x)=0c(x,x)=0c(x,x)=0. Then

L‾cϵ(α,β)→Lc(α,β)(ϵ→0),\overline{\mathcal L}_c^\epsilon(\alpha,\beta) \to \mathcal L_c(\alpha,\beta) \qquad (\epsilon\to0),Lcϵ​(α,β)→Lc​(α,β)(ϵ→0),
(57)

and

L‾cϵ(α,β)→−12∫c  d(α−β)⊗ d(α−β)(ϵ→+∞).\overline{\mathcal L}_c^\epsilon(\alpha,\beta) \to - \frac12 \int c\,\d(\alpha-\beta)\otimes\d(\alpha-\beta) \qquad (\epsilon\to+\infty).Lcϵ​(α,β)→−21​∫cd(α−β)⊗d(α−β)(ϵ→+∞).
(58)
Remark: Large-Temperature Hilbertian Limit

If −c-c−c defines a conditionally positive definite kernel, the large-temperature limit is the square of a Hilbertian kernel norm. A typical example is c(x,y)=∥x−y∥pc(x,y)=\norm{x-y}^pc(x,y)=∥x−y∥p for 0<p<20<p<20<p<2, which corresponds to the energy-distance kernel.

Proposition: Non-Negativity of Sinkhorn Divergences

If k(x,y)=e−c(x,y)/ϵk(x,y)=e^{-c(x,y)/\epsilon}k(x,y)=e−c(x,y)/ϵ is positive definite, then L‾cϵ(α,β)≥0\overline{\mathcal L}_c^\epsilon(\alpha,\beta)\ge0Lcϵ​(α,β)≥0.

Proof Sketch

Use the optimal self-potentials for (α,α)(\alpha,\alpha)(α,α) and (β,β)(\beta,\beta)(β,β) as a suboptimal pair in the dual problem between α\alphaα and β\betaβ. After rewriting with α~=efα,α/ϵα\tilde\alpha=e^{f_{\alpha,\alpha}/\epsilon}\alphaα~=efα,α​/ϵα and β~=efβ,β/ϵβ\tilde\beta=e^{f_{\beta,\beta}/\epsilon}\betaβ~​=efβ,β​/ϵβ, one obtains

1ϵL‾cϵ(α,β)≥1−⟨α~,β~⟩k.\frac{1}{\epsilon} \overline{\mathcal L}_c^\epsilon(\alpha,\beta) \ge 1-\langle \tilde\alpha,\tilde\beta\rangle_k.ϵ1​Lcϵ​(α,β)≥1−⟨α~,β~​⟩k​.
(59)

The self Sinkhorn fixed-point equations imply ∥α~∥k=∥β~∥k=1\norm{\tilde\alpha}_k=\norm{\tilde\beta}_k=1∥α~∥k​=∥∥​β~​∥∥​k​=1, so Cauchy--Schwarz gives the result.

Remark: Strict Positivity

Under additional assumptions on the kernel, L‾cϵ(α,β)=0\overline{\mathcal L}_c^\epsilon(\alpha,\beta)=0Lcϵ​(α,β)=0 implies α=β\alpha=\betaα=β, and the debiased divergence metrizes convergence in law.

References¶
  1. Sinkhorn, R. (1964). A relationship between arbitrary positive matrices and doubly stochastic matrices. The Annals of Mathematical Statistics, 35(2), 876–879. 10.1214/aoms/1177703591
  2. Sinkhorn, R., & Knopp, P. (1967). Concerning nonnegative matrices and doubly stochastic matrices. Pacific Journal of Mathematics, 21(2), 343–348. 10.2140/pjm.1967.21.343
  3. Sinkhorn, R. (1967). Diagonal equivalence to matrices with prescribed row and column sums. American Mathematical Monthly, 74, 402–405.

  8. Knight, P. A. (2008). The Sinkhorn–Knopp algorithm: convergence and applications. SIAM Journal on Matrix Analysis and Applications, 30(1), 261–275.
